act: Industrial Disputes Act 1947 / Factories Act 1948 / Payment of Wages Act 1936
description: Labour and employment laws governing worker rights, wages, termination, and workplace safety in India.

sections.title: Dismissal of Individual Workman as Industrial Dispute
sections.text: Where any employer discharges, dismisses, retrenches or otherwise terminates the services of an individual workman, any dispute or difference between that workman and his employer connected with, or arising out of, such discharge, dismissal, retrenchment or termination shall be deemed to be an industrial dispute.
sections.simplified: If you are fired or your services are terminated by your employer, it is considered an industrial dispute under law and you can raise it for resolution.

sections.title: Reference of Disputes to Tribunals
sections.text: Where the appropriate Government is of the opinion that any industrial dispute exists or is apprehended, it may at any time refer the dispute to a Board, Court, Labour Court, Tribunal, or National Tribunal for adjudication.
sections.simplified: If there is an industrial dispute, the government can refer it to a Labour Court or Tribunal for resolution.

sections.title: Conditions Precedent to Retrenchment of Workmen
sections.text: No workman employed in any industry who has been in continuous service for not less than one year shall be retrenched unless: (a) one month's notice in writing or wages in lieu, (b) compensation equivalent to 15 days' average pay for every completed year of service, (c) notice served on the appropriate Government.
sections.simplified: If you have worked for at least 1 year, your employer cannot fire you without: giving 1 month notice (or pay), paying 15 days salary per year of service as compensation, and notifying the government.

sections.title: Conditions Precedent to Closure
sections.text: An employer who intends to close down an undertaking of an industrial establishment employing not less than 100 workmen shall apply for prior permission at least 90 days before the intended date of closure to the appropriate Government.
sections.simplified: Companies with 100+ workers must get government permission at least 90 days before shutting down. They cannot just close suddenly.

sections.title: Recovery of Money Due from an Employer
sections.text: Where any money is due to a workman from an employer under a settlement, award or under the provisions of Chapter VA or Chapter VB, the workman may make an application to the appropriate Government for the recovery of such money.
sections.simplified: If your employer owes you money (unpaid salary, compensation, etc.), you can apply to the government for recovery of that money.

sections.title: Health and Safety Provisions (Factories Act)
sections.text: Every factory shall be kept clean and free from effluvia. The factory shall ensure adequate ventilation, temperature regulation, prevention of dust and fumes, artificial humidification, overcrowding prevention, and proper lighting for workers.
sections.simplified: Your workplace (factory) must be clean, well-ventilated, properly lit, and free from harmful dust or fumes. Your employer is legally required to maintain these conditions.
sections.punishment: Fine up to Rs. 1 lakh, imprisonment up to 2 years for repeat offences

sections.title: Weekly Hours and Overtime (Factories Act)
sections.text: No adult worker shall be required or allowed to work in a factory for more than 48 hours in any week. No worker shall work for more than 9 hours in any day. Where a worker works overtime, he shall be entitled to wages at the rate of twice his ordinary rate of wages.
sections.simplified: You cannot be forced to work more than 48 hours per week or 9 hours per day. If you work overtime, you must be paid double your normal wage.

sections.title: Responsibility for Payment of Wages (Payment of Wages Act)
sections.text: Every employer shall be responsible for the payment of wages to persons employed by him. Wages shall be paid before the expiry of the 7th day after the last day of the wage period in establishments employing less than 1000 persons, and before the 10th day in other cases.
sections.simplified: Your employer must pay your salary by the 7th of each month (for companies with less than 1000 workers) or by the 10th (for larger companies). Delay in payment is illegal.

sections.title: Deductions from Wages
sections.text: Deductions from the wages of an employed person shall be made only in accordance with the provisions of this Act. Authorized deductions include: fines (max 3% of wages), absence from duty, damage to goods (after inquiry), services/accommodation provided, income tax, court orders, and provident fund contributions.
sections.simplified: Your employer cannot randomly cut your salary. Only specific deductions are allowed by law, such as tax, provident fund, fines (max 3% of salary), and court orders. Any other deduction is illegal.

sections.title: Claims by Workers
sections.text: The appropriate Government may appoint Labour Commissioner or authority to hear and decide claims arising out of deductions from wages or delay in payment of wages. Every application shall be presented within 12 months from the date on which the deduction was made or wages became due.
sections.simplified: If your employer deducts salary illegally or delays payment, you can file a claim with the Labour Commissioner within 12 months.

sections.title: Minimum Wages (Minimum Wages Act)
sections.text: The appropriate Government shall fix minimum rates of wages in respect of scheduled employments. The minimum wages may consist of a basic rate of wages with a special allowance (cost of living), or a basic rate with or without cost of living allowance, and the cash value of essential commodities.
sections.simplified: The government sets minimum wages for various types of jobs. Your employer must pay you at least the minimum wage set for your type of work. Paying less than minimum wage is illegal.
sections.punishment: Imprisonment up to 6 months, fine up to Rs. 500, or both

sections.title: Employee State Insurance Benefits
sections.text: An insured person or (in the case of death) dependants shall be entitled to the following benefits: (a) sickness benefit, (b) maternity benefit, (c) disablement benefit, (d) dependants' benefit, (e) medical benefit, (f) funeral expenses. These benefits are available to employees earning up to Rs. 21,000 per month.
sections.simplified: If your salary is up to Rs. 21,000/month and your employer is registered under ESI, you are entitled to free medical care, sick leave pay, maternity benefits, and disability compensation.

sections.title: Sexual Harassment at Workplace (Prevention, Prohibition & Redressal Act 2013)
sections.text: Sexual harassment includes: (i) physical contact and advances, (ii) demand or request for sexual favours, (iii) sexually coloured remarks, (iv) showing pornography, (v) any other unwelcome physical, verbal or non-verbal conduct of sexual nature. Every employer shall constitute an Internal Complaints Committee.
sections.simplified: Sexual harassment at work includes unwanted touching, requests for sexual favours, dirty jokes, or showing inappropriate content. Every workplace must have an Internal Complaints Committee (ICC) where you can file complaints.
sections.punishment: Action as per service rules; employer face fine up to Rs. 50,000

sections.title: Complaints and Inquiry Process
sections.text: Any aggrieved woman may make a complaint of sexual harassment to the Internal Complaints Committee within 3 months from the date of incident. The ICC shall complete the inquiry within 90 days and submit its findings and recommendations to the employer.
sections.simplified: You must file your sexual harassment complaint within 3 months of the incident. The Internal Complaints Committee must complete their investigation within 90 days and recommend action.

sections.title: Gratuity (Payment of Gratuity Act 1972)
sections.text: Gratuity shall be payable to an employee on the termination of his employment after he has rendered continuous service for not less than five years. For every completed year of service, gratuity = 15 days wages × years of service. Maximum limit: Rs. 20 lakh.
sections.simplified: After 5 years of continuous service, you are entitled to gratuity when you leave. Calculation: 15 days salary × number of years worked. Maximum gratuity is Rs. 20 lakh. This applies even if you resign.
sections.punishment: Imprisonment up to 1 year, fine up to Rs. 10,000, or both
